text: The surgeon uses a robotic needle driver to place stitches, leaving them loose at the end. The needle driver is pointed at the hole, allowing the suction cannula to be directed towards it when inserted through the port. The bedside assistant can insert the cannula with or without a finger covering the opening.
The surgeon observes the lateral edge of the mesh, which appears slightly curled. The surgeon presses down on the mesh to flatten it, ensuring it lies flat against the tissue.

The surgeon demonstrates pushing down the lifted edge of the flap. If unsuccessful, a hole is created near the area for further dissection to properly position the mesh. After removing the cannula, the pocket is observed to remain desufflated, followed by running the V-lock suture back two throws before cutting it.
The surgeon demonstrates the evacuation of a bilateral angular hernia pocket using a large bore cannula, which rapidly desufflates the pocket without the need for a needle insertion.

The surgeon observes the mesh slightly lifted and proceeds to reposition it. If the mesh is significantly elevated, the assistant removes the cannula to allow CO2 to re-enter the pocket for readjustment. Once the cannula is removed, the surgeon tightens the suture. Occasionally, CO2 re-enters due to a heavy peritoneum or a small hole.

The surgeon completes the procedure without risk of injuring the inferior gastric vessels, using a fourth arm for a scrotal hernia. The flap is reconfigured due to tissue loss, ensuring it is watertight or airtight. The needle driver remains in the right lower quadrant, facilitating rapid evacuation of the pocket.

The surgeon checks the placement of the mesh to ensure it provides good coverage around the scrotal hernia. The pocket is evacuated using a suction cannula, which is a simple and cost-effective method.
